eia_classification.norway_overview: Norway's oil blends are generally light and sweet to medium in density and sulfur content (0.0%-0.2% low to 0.2%-0.9% medium sulfur by weight). Ekofisk, Oseberg, and Troll blends produced offshore Norway. The three Norwegian streams account for ~55% of North Sea Brent crude oil loadings (~1.2 million b/d total).

brent_benchmark_bfoet.global_pricing_share: >65% of global physical crude is priced off BFOET

Statfjord_Blend.sulfur_note: low-sulfur, slightly lower than Brent Blend
Statfjord_Blend.source: Equinor / Oil & Gas Journal assay update 2023-11-29

Gullfaks_Blend.source: Oil & Gas Journal Guide to World Crudes

Grane_Blend.classification: heavy sour (first heavy crude on NCS)
Grane_Blend.source: Statoil/Equinor assay via Oil & Gas Journal

Johan_Sverdrup.market_notes: Popular with complex refiners; firm favorite in Asia (esp. China). Comparable to Grane in API and to Forties in sulfur. Used for IMO 2020 bunker blending.

Forties_Blend.source: S&P Global Platts / Argus / EIA Today In Energy
Forties_Blend.note: UK grade, not NCS, but a Brent benchmark component

Ekofisk_Blend.classification: classic light sweet North Sea crude
Ekofisk_Blend.note: Specific assay numbers not retrieved in search results; Ekofisk is part of BFOET basket and is broadly characterized as light sweet (API ~35-40, sulfur ~0.1-0.4%).

Oseberg_Blend.classification: classic light sweet North Sea crude
Oseberg_Blend.note: Specific assay numbers not retrieved in search results; part of BFOET basket; broadly light sweet.

Heidrun.classification: not retrieved in this search round
Heidrun.note: Norwegian Sea field; assay available from Equinor / ExxonMobil libraries but not surfaced in search results.

Snorre.note: Commingled into Statfjord Blend (API 39.1, low sulfur)

Goliat.classification: not retrieved in this search round
Goliat.note: Barents Sea field operated by Vaar Energi (Eni). Assay not surfaced in this search; deeper queries needed.

primary_assay_libraries.name: Equinor Crude Oil Assays
primary_assay_libraries.note: Equinor publishes commercial assays for all operated streams; periodically updated (e.g. Statfjord updated Nov 2023; Troll updated Aug 2022).

primary_assay_libraries.name: ExxonMobil Crude Trading Assays
primary_assay_libraries.note: Downloadable assay PDFs by blend name.

primary_assay_libraries.name: BP Crude Assays Library
covers: BP-marketed grades including UK and NCS streams
primary_assay_libraries.note: Technical downloads via BP Supply, Trading & Shipping.

primary_assay_libraries.name: Oil & Gas Journal: Guide to World Crudes
covers: All major Norwegian assays at update events
primary_assay_libraries.note: Editorial summaries of operator assay releases.

primary_assay_libraries.name: S&P Global Platts EMEA Crude Methodology
covers: BFOET basket spec, quality adjustment factors
primary_assay_libraries.note: Defines API/sulfur quality adjustments used in Dated Brent assessment.

gaps_and_next_steps: Numeric API/sulfur for Ekofisk, Oseberg, Heidrun, Goliat not directly surfaced; must hit Equinor/ConocoPhillips assay PDFs and ExxonMobil library directly.

BP Statistical Review of World Energy does NOT publish per-field crude assays; it only covers reserves/production/consumption aggregates. For per-field quality, primary sources are operator assay PDFs (Equinor/ExxonMobil/BP Trading/ConocoPhillips/Vaar Energi/Aker BP).
IEA does not publish per-field crude assays either; their oil quality material is aggregated at country/region level.
EIA Company Level Imports data file does contain per-cargo API gravity and sulfur for crude imported into the US, including Norwegian cargoes; this is a usable historical time series.
key_takeaways_for_ncs_decline_quality_an: NCS crude is bimodal: (a) light sweet Brent-like streams (Statfjord ~39 API, Gullfaks ~40 API, Troll, Oseberg, Ekofisk, all <0.4% S) and (b) heavier sour streams (Grane 23.7 API / 0.73% S, Johan Sverdrup 28.7 API / 0.8% S).

Light sweet NCS volume share is declining as legacy Brent fields decline and heavier fields (Grane 2003, Johan Sverdrup 2019) ramp; this is the documented North Sea quality shift driving customer base changes.
Light sweet grades historically priced at premium to Dated Brent; heavier sour grades (Grane, JS) trade at discount but find strong demand from complex Asian refiners.
For decline_quality model: API gravity is a usable single-axis quality proxy across NCS; light sweet vs. heavy sour split is the main quality signal that maps onto field-level decline curves.
